data.title: a16z raised $15 billion, claiming it would make America win.
data.description:
Author: Curry, Deep Tide TechFlow

Last Friday, a16z announced the completion of a $15 billion fundraising round.

Note that this is fundraising, not investment. LPs entrust them with money so they can invest in others.

How staggering is this number?

In 2025, all VCs in the US raised a total of $66.1 billion, the lowest in eight years. a16z alone took nearly 20%.

The industry is going through a downturn, and they are stockpiling.

But why are LPs willing to entrust them with money during this harsh winter?

Perhaps because they have a track record of making money during downturns.

In 2009, they invested in Facebook, just after the financial crisis, and nobody dared to invest. In 2013, they invested in Coinbase, when most people thought Bitcoin was a geek toy. In May 2022, Bitcoin fell by 55%, Coinbase's stock price fell by 80%, and a16z was still raising a $4.5 billion crypto fund.

At the time, the comments section was full of laughter at them taking over the losses.

Last year, The Information reported that the fund's returns had soared. The reason was simple: their investment in Solana rose from $8 to $180 at that time.

"Be greedy when others are fearful" is often just motivational rhetoric. But if you're greedy every time you're fearful, and you always win, it becomes a kind of credit record. Returning to the $15 billion, how will a16z spend it? $6.75 billion will be invested in growth companies, reinforcing projects that have already succeeded and are poised for further expansion. $1.7 billion will be invested in application layers, $1.7 billion in underlying technologies, and $700 million in biopharmaceuticals. The remaining $1.176 billion will be invested in a theme called "American Dynamism." The direct translation is "American Vitality," which sounds like a slogan created by a high-end think tank. Looking at what they've already invested in under this theme reveals the true meaning: Enabling America to build again. Build what? Weapons. Companies in this portfolio, such as Anduril (autonomous weapon systems), Shield AI (military drones), Saronic (unmanned warships), and Castelion (hypersonic missiles), share a common thread: their largest customer is the Pentagon. a16z himself stated that if a war broke out with China in the Taiwan Strait, the US missile arsenal would be depleted in 8 days, and it would take 3 years to replenish it. In the eyes of the Americans, this isn't alarmist; it's business. The US military-industrial complex is aging; Lockheed Martin and similar companies are too slow and expensive, and the Pentagon needs new suppliers. a16z is betting on this gap, using VC money to incubate a batch of "software-defined weapons" companies, which will then be sold to the Department of Defense once they mature. $1.176 billion isn't a large sum, but it represents a bet on a judgment. The US is rebuilding its manufacturing industry, starting with the military. Betting on the military requires more than just money; it also requires connections. a16z has that. In late 2024, Marc Andreessen described himself as an "unpaid intern" at DOGE (Department for Government Efficiency), helping the department recruit, and reportedly spending half his time at Mar-a-Lago advising Trump. DOGE was disbanded early last November, but a16z's network remained. Their first employee, Scott Kupor, is now the Director of the Office of Personnel Management in the United States. Trump just said this week that the defense budget will be increased to $1.5 trillion next year. Many VCs invest in the defense industry, but very few can simultaneously invest in companies and influence policy. This may be one of a16z's true moats—not just investing, but also participating in rule-making. They invested in missile companies while simultaneously helping the government decide who gets contracts. It feels a bit like being both referee and player. You could call it a conflict of interest, or you could call it resource integration. Anyway, LPs don't care; they only care about returns. Many people know a16z because of Crypto. In this $15 billion deal, Crypto wasn't listed separately; it was crammed into the $3 billion "Others" category. Has crypto been abandoned? No. Ben Horowitz clearly stated in his blog, "AI and Crypto are the key architectures of the future." However, for a16z, Crypto no longer needs a separate fund. Their first Crypto fund in 2018 was $350 million. By 2022, it had grown to $4.5 billion. Now? They invest directly from their main fund, putting it in the same pool as AI, military, and energy. What does this signify? This shows that in their eyes, Crypto has transformed from a "new track" into infrastructure. Exchanges are infrastructure, public chains are infrastructure, and DeFi protocols are also infrastructure. Like AWS, Nvidia, and missiles, they are all underlying technologies. Previously, VCs invested in Crypto; now they invest in infrastructure. The landscape has broadened. For the Crypto industry, this is actually good news. Being categorized as "other" might seem like a downgrade, but it's more like graduation. It means this concept no longer needs separate explanation; limited partners (LPs) understand it, and Western regulators are beginning to accept it. Of course, this also means Crypto projects will have to compete with AI and military projects for the same funding pools. The competition is becoming more intense. Meanwhile, Ben also wrote a sentence on his blog that Sequoia might not be too happy to hear: "As a leader in US venture capital, the fate of new technologies rests partly on our shoulders." Sequoia has been in the industry for 50 years, while a16z has only been for 16. Yet both now manage around $90 billion, tied for first place globally. Why? In essence, VC sells two things: vision and resources. Vision is hard to prove. You might say you have a good eye, but you'll have to wait ten years to see. Resources are different; they can be accumulated. What a16z has been doing all these years is building up its resources. They have the strongest content team in the industry, producing podcasts, blogs, and newsletters with output comparable to media companies. Before entrepreneurs even receive funding, they've already been thoroughly influenced by a16z's worldview. They have connections in Washington. It's not just about knowing a few politicians; it's about directly placing people in the government. They also have the advantage of scale. Managing $90 billion in funds, you can write a $1 billion check to SpaceX—smaller funds simply can't do that. It's not just about making accurate investments; it's about making yourself irreplaceable. Entrepreneurs approach you not because you have a lot of money, but because you can help them secure government contracts. LPs approach you not because of high returns, but because others don't have your policy influence. This approach is difficult for other VCs to replicate. Of course, there are risks involved. a16z's current strategy is partly tied to the fate of the United States. If their predictions of AI success, military industry growth, and the revival of American manufacturing are wrong, a large portion of their $15 billion investment will be lost. a16z is betting not just on the technology cycle, but on the political cycle. This cycle is arguably even harder to predict than the technology cycle. However, the fact that LPs are willing to entrust them with $15 billion indicates that the market believes in this judgment. Or rather, in an uncertain world, a16z offers a kind of certainty: We know how to turn money into influence, and then influence into returns. We invest when others dare not, we bet when others don't understand. Then we wait for the cycle to turn around and reap the rewards of trust. Therefore, you can understand this $15 billion as a vote of confidence from LPs to a16z. Next, we'll see how a16z invests in the US.

data.title: Uniswap Enables Fee Switch: From Governance Tokens to Value Capture
data.description:
Author: Tanay Ved Source: coinmetrics Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Key Points

  • Uniswap's fee switch deeply binds the UNI token to protocol usage through a token burn mechanism. Protocol fees will now be used to reduce the circulating supply of UNI, transforming UNI from a simple governance token into an asset that can directly capture value.

  • Initial data shows that the protocol's annualized fees are approximately $26 million, representing a revenue multiple of approximately 207 times.

  • The current annual burning of approximately 4 million UNI tokens, within its $5.4 billion valuation, reflects the market's high growth expectations for the token. DeFi is gradually shifting towards a fee-linked token model. Mechanisms such as token burning, staker reward distribution, and delegated voting lock-up are all designed to better balance the interests of token holders and the protocol economy, which will reshape the valuation logic of the entire industry. In late 2025, Uniswap's governance approved the "UNI Integration" proposal, officially launching the long-awaited protocol "fee switch." This is one of the most influential token economic reforms among blue-chip decentralized finance projects since 2020. With the market increasingly focused on actual returns and sustainable fee-driven value capture models, this move is timely. The implementation of the fee switch establishes a more direct link between the UNI token and the revenue and trading activity of this leading decentralized exchange in the crypto space. This article will delve into Uniswap's token economic model after the implementation of the fee switch, assess the dynamic relationship between token burning and fees and its impact on UNI's valuation, and explore the profound significance of this change for the entire decentralized finance (DeFi) industry. The Dilemma of the Disconnect Between Decentralized Finance Tokens and Protocol Value One of the core pain points in the decentralized finance (DeFi) field lies in the value disconnect between high-quality protocols and weak tokens. Many protocols have achieved mature product-market fit, boasting high usage rates and stable revenue, but their issued tokens often only serve governance functions, with token holders rarely directly enjoying protocol cash flow benefits. In this market environment, capital flows into other asset classes such as Bitcoin, public chain native tokens, and memes, while the trading prices of most DeFi tokens are decoupled from the actual growth of the protocols. Uniswap launched on the Ethereum network in November 2018. It is a decentralized exchange focused on ERC-20 token swaps, eliminating the need for order books and intermediaries. Shortly after, in 2020, the UNI token was officially issued, positioned as a governance token. This model became the mainstream choice for blue-chip decentralized finance projects at the time, with platforms such as Aave, Compound, and Curve primarily using their issued tokens for governance and incentive purposes.

    Data Source: Coin Metrics Network Data Pro

    After multiple version iterations, Uniswap has become a core component of on-chain financial infrastructure, processing billions of dollars in transactions daily and generating substantial fee revenues for liquidity providers. However, like most decentralized finance governance tokens, UNI token holders cannot directly share in the protocol's revenue, leading to a growing disconnect between the protocol's cash flow and the economic interests of token holders.

    ... Under the previous mechanism, the value generated by the protocol primarily flowed to liquidity providers, lenders and borrowers, and the relevant development team, while token holders only received governance rights and inflationary rewards. It was this contradiction between "pure governance tokens" and "value capture" that laid the foundation for Uniswap's fee on/off switch and the "UNI integration" proposal. This proposal explicitly links the value of the UNI token to the protocol's usage rate, effectively balancing the economic interests of token holders and exchanges.

    Uniswap Fee Switch: Fee and Burn Mechanism Analysis

    Following the adoption of the UNI integrated governance proposal, the protocol implemented the following changes:

    1. Activating the Protocol Fee and UNI Burn Mechanism: The protocol's "fee switch" is activated, injecting protocol-level fees generated by the Ethereum mainnet V2 and V3 liquidity pools into the UNI token burn mechanism. This move transforms UNI's economic model from a pure governance model to a deflationary value capture model based on protocol usage and token supply.

    2. Executing Treasury Token Retrospective Burn: 100 million UNI tokens were burned from the treasury in a single transaction to compensate for the losses incurred by token holders who had not participated in fee sharing over the years.

    3. Executing Retrospective Burn of Treasury Tokens: 100 million UNI tokens were burned from the treasury in a single transaction to compensate for the losses incurred by token holders who had not participated in fee sharing over the years.

    4. **Collect Unichain Revenue:** All transaction fees generated by the Unichain sorter, after deducting Ethereum mainnet data costs and the 15% share paid to Optimism, will be incorporated into the aforementioned burn-driven value capture mechanism. **Adjust Organizational Incentive Mechanisms:** Most of the Uniswap Foundation's functions will be integrated into Uniswap Labs, with an annual growth budget of 20 million UNI. This will encourage Uniswap Labs to focus on protocol promotion while reducing its fee share from user interface, wallet, and application programming interface (API) transactions to zero.

    Data source: Uniswap UNIfication

    Currently, Uniswap adopts a "pipeline" operation model, managing the release and conversion process of assets (such as UNI token burning) through dedicated smart contracts.

    The specific process is as follows: transaction fees generated on V2, V3, and Unichain → a portion of the fees go to the protocol (the remainder is allocated to liquidity providers) → the protocol fees on each public chain are all deposited into a dedicated vault smart contract called TokenJar → assets in the vault can only be withdrawn by destroying UNI tokens through the Firepit smart contract.

    Data source: Coin Metrics ATLAS

    According to Coin Metrics ATLAS data, in the first 12 days after the fee switch was activated, a considerable amount of protocol fees flowed into the system.

    The chart below tracks the estimated daily protocol fees (in USD) and the cumulative total, visually demonstrating the monetization efficiency of Uniswap trading volume under the initial fee switch configuration—in just 12 days, protocol-level fees have accumulated to approximately $800,000. If current market conditions remain stable, the annualized revenue for the protocol is projected to reach $26 million to $27 million. However, actual revenue will depend on market trading activity and the progress of the fee mechanism's adoption across various liquidity pools and public chains.

    Data source: Coin Metrics ATLAS

    The token burn data in the above chart shows how transaction fees are converted into a reduction in the circulating supply of UNI (excluding the 100 million treasury retroactively burned tokens). The cumulative amount of UNI tokens burned has reached approximately 100.17 million (approximately US$557 million), representing 10.1% of the initial circulating supply of 1 billion tokens.

data.title: After AI lowers the skill threshold, where will humanity's competitive advantage lie?
data.description:
Author: Haotian
After experiencing Vibe Coding for a while, during my hospital stay, I kept thinking: When AI eliminates the barriers to "skills," where exactly is our moat as "human beings"?
1) Content is no longer important; "perspective" is the scarce asset.
When the cost of content production is infinitesimally close to zero, the information barrier is completely broken down, but this does not mean the demise of "creators." On the contrary, there will be a victory for "subjectivity."
Our attitude towards all content as "human beings" and our personal experience of the world become particularly important. This is what the author means when he says your brand = your story + worldview.
Content can be standardized by algorithms, but the filtering mechanism of "perspective," with its warmth, bias, and unique experiences, is where the biggest premium lies in the future.
2) Ideas become products; the friction damping of execution is disappearing.
Previously, creating data dashboards and research tools required various skill levels. Now, a few prompts can turn ideas into reality, improving efficiency, sharing with friends, or even directly monetizing them as products. We are being forced to transform from "consumers" to "product managers." The era of "everyone is a product manager," which has been touted for many years, is truly arriving. For example, if I discover my Claude account has been inexplicably banned this morning, and I heavily rely on the Project feature, I immediately redesign a personalized front-end using a local database and Claude Code to replace the original web version of Claude. Using my own ideas to trigger various underlying models, all sorts of strange ideas can be productized for my own use (this awareness and ability are crucial). 3) Say goodbye to the era of specialized specialization and strive to be a pioneer in the "generalist era." The industrial age turned people into cogs in a machine (Specialist) because that was the most efficient way. But in the AI ​​era, AI is the most efficient "expert." It can write more standardized code and calculate data faster than you. This is when the "generalist's" golden age arrives. Don't strive for perfection; once you fall into the trap of perfectionism by optimizing details, the flaws in Vibe coding will make it pale in comparison to mature, industrialized software. What we need to do is not compete with programmers in code elegance, but rather compete with time in bringing ideas to fruition. It's better to spend an hour having AI rewrite it from a different perspective than spending a week optimizing a single feature. In the AI ​​era, "completion" is far more important than "perfection."

data.title: Why do currency-related cases get stuck at the "civil remedy stage"?
data.description:
Introduction: The risks of cryptocurrency-related cases are concentrated in the "relief phase"

When handling cryptocurrency-related cases, you will repeatedly encounter a very typical emotion of the parties involved:

"I know I've been scammed, the money has indeed been transferred, and I can see it on the blockchain, so why is no one taking action and I can't get it back?"

The problem is often not at the factual level, but at the procedural level—

The clearer the facts, the easier it is for the parties involved to mistakenly believe that "relief can be initiated"; however, in cryptocurrency-related cases, whether relief can be initiated depends on three things: the nature of the crime, jurisdiction, and whether the evidence can be put into practice.

The nature of cryptocurrency disputes has changed in recent years: Early on, it was mostly about "theft and absconding"; now, it's more about seemingly legitimate structures (listing services, U-merchant exchanges, NFT investment platforms), but with more complex asset paths, more dispersed entities, and more frequent cross-border involvement. Therefore, when cases reach the "relief stage," they often encounter three obstacles: The assets have value, but their nature and transaction relationships are not easily determined quickly: Is it property, contractual subject matter, investment rights, or a "tool" in a suspected criminal chain? Cross-border and multi-entity structures make jurisdiction and evidence collection a real challenge: The blockchain is here, the exchange is there, the server is overseas, and the person has absconded? Civil and criminal matters are mutually constrained: Once deemed "potentially involved in criminal activity," civil matters can easily be stalled or even transferred to other jurisdictions, and the pace of redress is completely out of the parties' control. The following three typical cases will clarify "why the process is stalled" and "what a lawyer can do." Real Case Review: Case 1: Cross-border "Listing Service Fee" Scam A company from country H planned to list its token on an exchange in country S and contacted a Chinese salesperson at the exchange. They agreed to pay 800,000 USDT as a listing service fee. After payment, the salesperson disappeared, and the exchange claimed the salesperson had resigned, and the service fee had not been received. Key Obstacles in Case Progression The case involves significant cross-border elements, making it difficult to directly initiate civil remedies; regarding procedural choice, the principle of criminal priority requires resolving the issue of whether to file a case first. The cross-border nature of crypto assets presents practical difficulties for evidence collection and jurisdiction, such as wallet transaction records being distributed across different blockchains and exchange servers located overseas. There is disagreement regarding the nature of the conduct: whether it constitutes criminal fraud or civil breach of contract directly determines which procedure the case proceeds to and whether remedies can be initiated. What can lawyers do? The first step isn't to rush into writing about "how you were scammed," but rather to outline "how the money went": the transfer chain, wallet address, timeline, the other party's identity documents, communication records, and exchange feedback. Prepare two sets of statements simultaneously: For criminal cases: emphasize the leverage of territorial/personal jurisdiction and stress the key fact of "intent to illegally possess" (inducement, fabricated identity/authority, service promises, refusal to fulfill obligations, disappearance, etc.). For civil cases: compress the dispute into "service fee payment—service not fulfilled—unfounded possession by the other party," providing a foundation for subsequent negotiations/asset preservation. Don't treat "exchange replies" as conclusions, but as entry points for evidence: it's common for exchanges to deny responsibility, but lawyers should turn them into clues about "the other party's internal management, authority, and business relationships." Case 2: USDT Exchange Scam An investor met an "investment advisor" online and was recommended to exchange USDT through a U-merchant. The investor transferred over three million yuan to multiple accounts, but the funds never arrived. The U-merchant was subsequently arrested, but since it only provided exchange services and had no direct connection to the upstream fraud ring, the police ultimately terminated the investigation. Key Obstacles in Case Progress First, conduct a "traceability assessment," then discuss the path forward. This assessment is harsh, but necessary: ​​Which accounts can still be frozen? Which entities can be located? What evidence can be used to close the loop?

Break down the "flow of funds" into two parallel tracks:

  • Bank side: Transfer chain, identity of receiving account, destination of funds (whether concentrated, whether short-term dispersed).

  • On-chain side: Whether there are nodes related to aggregation, collection, cross-chain, and exchange entry related to known wallets.

The core variable in these types of cases is often not "whether a lawsuit can be filed/initiated," but "whether asset control can be achieved at key nodes." We will explain the feasibility and risks of each node to clients simultaneously during the process, ensuring that decisions are based on feasibility.

Case 3: NFT Investment Scam A client purchased a series of high-value NFTs through an online platform, which claimed that these NFTs could generate future art dividends and rare digital rights. After the client paid approximately 5 million RMB, the platform suddenly shut down, the website became inaccessible, and the person in charge disappeared. Subsequent investigations revealed that the NFT smart contract code contained a backdoor, allowing assets to be transferred at will. Key Obstacles in Case Progress As a derivative right of digital assets, NFTs possess both investment and trading attributes, and their legal characterization remains highly uncertain under existing rules. The platform's entity and smart contracts are highly anonymous, and their cross-border deployment presents real obstacles to asset tracking, entity identification, and jurisdiction. Even with technical tracking through contract logs, on-chain records, or IP information, cross-chain assets often involve multiple jurisdictions, making actual recovery extremely difficult. Expanding the practical perspective: Translating the technical facts into legal terms: a contract backdoor means control is not in the customer's hands; "free transfer" corresponds to the crucial factual basis of "illegal possession intent." Don't just focus on on-chain evidence: bank statements, recharge records, platform promotional promises, profit-sharing mechanisms, chat logs, contract terms, and backend screenshots are often more persuasive to law enforcement than "on-chain analysis reports." It's also crucial to explain the probability of recovery beforehand: a contract backdoor + cross-chain + anonymity structure essentially maximizes the difficulty of asset recovery; even criminal charges may not "save" the assets, but at least control of key nodes can be secured. The Core Reasons for Obstruction of Civil Remedies Looking back at the three cases mentioned above, it can be seen that although the types of cases are different, they all encountered highly similar institutional obstacles in the civil path after entering the remedy stage. 1. The Principle of Criminal Priority Acts involving criminal offenses must first be investigated and prosecuted; civil litigation usually has to wait for the completion of criminal proceedings. Once a criminal judgment addresses property rights, a subsequent civil lawsuit will trigger the principle of "res judicata." If a civil case is transferred to the public security bureau and the court determines that the conduct constitutes a crime, the civil path is forcibly interrupted. 2. Difficulty in Cross-Border Accountability

  • The funds and suspects involved in the case are distributed across multiple countries, making cross-border evidence collection, investigation, and enforcement highly restricted.

  • Anonymity and programmability allow assets to be split and transferred in a short period of time, further weakening the possibility of recovery.

3. Complex Asset Identification and Behavior Characterization

  • Crypto assets can be payment tools or carry investment or derivative rights; differences in characterization directly affect the path to relief.

  • Even if civil proceedings are initiated, courts often find it difficult to support requests for restitution due to asset dispersion, insufficient evidence, or unclear application of law.

Practical Implications

Limited civil remedies are not only a procedural issue, but also a systemic constraint.

In currency-related cases, the criminal route remains the most realistic and feasible remedy. The core role of lawyers is to help clients rationally plan their path and avoid exhausting their only remedy in procedural choices.

Implications for lawyers: Don't just "pile up materials," focus on "path control."

Based on the issues exposed at different stages of the three cases mentioned above, the core capabilities of lawyers in currency-related cases can be summarized into three levels: front-end risk identification, evidence and structural control during the process, and a clear understanding of the boundaries of the system.

(I) Front-end: Identifying risks in advance, rather than remediating afterward. Legality assessment of transactions: Analyze whether they involve risks of illegal fundraising, fraud, or illegal business operations, focusing on whether the tokens have security characteristics. Asset attribute differentiation: Payment or functional tokens are more likely to be included in the "tradable property" framework; tokens with guaranteed returns are more likely to trigger criminal intervention. Cross-border structure prediction: Whether it involves overseas entities, exchanges, or wallet addresses directly determines the difficulty of subsequent accountability. (II) Process: Constructing a Judicially Acceptable Chain of Evidence Standardization of Contracts and Transaction Records: Clearly define the purpose of transactions and rights and obligations; systematically store on-chain transaction records and operation logs. Retention of Cross-Border Communication Evidence: Bank statements, platform emails, chat logs, etc.; attention should be paid to the time of formation and continuity. (III) Understanding Institutional Boundaries: Path Selection Itself is a Strategy Most cryptocurrency-related cases cannot bypass criminal procedures; the feasibility of criminal proceedings should be assessed first. Civil litigation is more of a supplementary tool for negotiation and fund recovery than a primary reliance. Lawyers need to clearly manage client expectations and avoid misinterpreting "procedure existence" as "inevitable outcome." (IV) Advanced Practice: Towards "Critical Judgments" Many cases may still get stuck even if you prepare the materials to the best of your ability. The reason is usually not the level of effort, but several "critical judgments": How exactly is the nature of the behavior understood (fraud vs. dispute; organizer vs. intermediary; beneficiary vs. tool); Can the risk structure be identified in advance (profit promises, collection nodes, transaction entry points, evidence of control); Have the window of opportunity been seized (freezing nodes, investigation nodes, entity locking nodes)? These judgments aren't written in the legal statutes, but they determine the course of a case. From "What can be done" to "When can it be done": Turning key judgments into reusable methods. Returning to the three cases mentioned earlier, a common thread emerges: Many cryptocurrency cases aren't "without rules," but rather there are gaps between the rules—characterization, jurisdiction, evidence, asset control—each of these can potentially halt the case's progress. More realistically, even if the lawyer prepares the materials perfectly, the case may still stall at a certain point—not due to a lack of ability, but because it encounters several types of "critical judgments": Is this fraud or a transaction dispute? Can the responsible party be identified? Is the asset control window still open? These judgments are difficult to explain with a single sentence of experience, nor can they be directly derived from a few legal provisions. It's more like a "craft" of case handling: the same facts can be presented in different ways – some can present them as grounds for prosecution, while others can only describe them as "suspected disputes." The difference often lies in how the evidence is organized, how the path is ordered, and how the key points are accurately identified. Therefore, the closed-door seminar/practical training we arranged in Zhengzhou this year wasn't about "repeating the concepts," but rather about breaking down these judgments and transforming them into working methods that lawyers can directly use: Focusing on the most easily misjudged and outcome-influencing points: when to prioritize criminal proceedings, when to pursue asset preservation, when to use civil litigation as a supplementary tool, and when to adjust strategies. Systematically dissecting the criminal entry points, jurisdictional leverage, asset control nodes, and the organization of the evidence loop; Clearly explaining the basic logic of the industry and transaction system, and also creating a reusable framework for the high-risk structures in cryptocurrency cases, the application of charges and defense approaches, and the methodologies for key case progression points. The goal is simple—to get you back into practice, able to handle consultations, and more reliably handle cryptocurrency criminal cases.

data.title: Crypto Trends in 2026: New Battlegrounds for Privacy, Decentralization, and Security
data.description:
Privacy will become the most important moat for the crypto world this year

Privacy is a key function for truly putting global finance on-chain. Unfortunately, almost all blockchains currently lack this feature. For most chains, privacy has always been an optional extra. Today, privacy alone is enough to make a chain stand out from the crowd.

More importantly, privacy can create an on-chain "lock-in effect," forming a unique network effect. This is especially true today, when performance advantage is no longer the sole competitive advantage.

If everything is public, migrating from one chain to another via cross-chain protocols is almost effortless. But when it comes to privacy, the situation is different: tokens can cross chains, but "secrets" are difficult to cross chains. There is always a risk of leakage when moving from a privacy chain to a public chain, or even between two privacy chains; for example, metadata such as transaction time and size may be tracked by external observers.

Compared to new chains that are highly homogenized and whose transaction fees may be driven to zero, blockchains with privacy features can have stronger network effects. The reality is that if a "general-purpose chain" lacks a mature ecosystem, killer applications, or distribution advantages, few people will choose to use or develop on it, let alone cultivate loyalty. On public chains, cross-chain transactions are easy for users; joining any chain makes little difference. However, on privacy chains, choosing which chain to join is crucial—once joined, users are unlikely to easily leave to avoid exposing themselves. This creates a "winner-takes-all" scenario. Because most real-world applications require privacy, a few privacy chains may control most of the value in the crypto world. This year's challenges for instant messaging applications are not just about resisting quantum computing, but also about decentralization. With the approach of quantum computing, many encryption-based communication applications (Apple, Signal, WhatsApp) are performing well. The problem is that every mainstream communication application relies on private servers we trust, servers that are easily shut down by governments, have backdoors implanted, or are forced to hand over data. What's the use of quantum encryption if a country can shut down servers directly, or if companies control the private server keys? Private servers mean "you have to trust me," while the absence of private servers means "you don't have to trust me." Communication doesn't need a company as an intermediary; we need open protocols, where we don't have to trust anyone. The way to achieve this is through decentralized networks: no private servers, no single application, all code is open source, and top-tier encryption (including quantum threat protection). In an open network, no individual, company, institution, or country can deprive us of our ability to communicate. Even if an application is shut down, hundreds of new versions will emerge the next day; shutting down a node will be immediately replaced by economic incentives (mechanisms like blockchain). When users control their messages like they control their own funds—possessing private keys—everything changes. Applications can come and go, but users always control their messages and identities. Ultimately, users truly own their messages, not just the applications themselves. This isn't just a matter of quantum encryption; it's a matter of "ownership" and "decentralization." Without these two elements, we've simply built an "unbreakable" encryption system that can be shut down at any time. "Privacy as a Service"—Making Privacy Infrastructure. Behind every model, intelligent agent, and automated system lies data. But today, most data pipelines—the data input to models or the outputs—are opaque, tamper-proof, and unauditable. This may not be a problem for some consumer applications, but industries like finance and healthcare require sensitive data to be kept confidential. This is also a significant obstacle for institutions looking to put real-world assets on-chain. So, how can we ensure privacy while making innovation secure, compliant, autonomous, and globally interoperable? Many approaches are being explored, with the author focusing on "data access control": Who controls sensitive data? How does data flow? Who or what can access it? Without access control, confidential data can only rely on centralized services or self-built systems—time-consuming, expensive, and hindering traditional financial institutions from fully utilizing on-chain data. As intelligent systems begin to browse, trade, and make decisions autonomously, users and institutions need cryptographic guarantees, not just "best-effort" trust. This is why we need "privacy as a service": providing programmable data access rules, client-side encryption, decentralized key management, specifying who can decrypt, under what conditions, and for how long… all rules are enforced on-chain. By combining verifiable data systems, privacy services can become a core public infrastructure of the internet, rather than just application-layer privacy patches, truly making privacy an infrastructure. Security testing will shift from "code is law" to "standards are law." Last year, even DeFi protocols with experienced teams, rigorous audits, and years of operational experience suffered hacking attacks. This exposed a disturbing fact: current security practices are still primarily experience-based and case-driven. This year, DeFi security needs to move from "vulnerability models" to "design-level attributes," and from "best-effort" to "principle-driven": Static/Pre-deployment (testing, auditing, formal verification): Systematically verify global invariants, rather than manually selected local conditions. Teams are now using AI tools to assist in proving and proposing invariants, significantly reducing manual costs. Dynamic/Post-Deployment (Runtime Monitoring, Execution-Time Enforcement): These invariants can be transformed into real-time guardrails; every transaction must meet the rules, otherwise it automatically rolls back. This means no longer assuming every vulnerability can be pre-discovered, but rather enforcing critical security attributes directly in the code. This is not just theoretical. In fact, to date, almost all known vulnerabilities trigger these checks at runtime, potentially preventing hacking attacks. Therefore, the once-popular "code is law" concept will evolve into "standards are law": even new attacks must meet system security attributes, leaving the remaining attacks either minor or extremely difficult to execute.

data.title: Who is the true god of Polymarket?
data.description:
Authors: Claire, Morris, Sunny, Hubble AI

Polymarket boasts numerous "god-like" addresses with single-trade profits exceeding $100,000. However, faced with hundreds of thousands of accounts, a core question consistently plagues participants: Is this replicable alpha, or unsustainable luck?

Existing leaderboards have a serious blind spot: they only show short-term results, failing to reveal the stability of strategies. To eliminate the element of luck, we bypassed simple leaderboards and directly analyzed 90,000 active addresses and 2 million settled transactions on-chain.

After removing the interference of floating profits, we discovered four brutally real "counterintuitive" patterns in the prediction market and redefined the selection criteria for copy trading.

TL;DR

  • Mid-frequency efficiency trap: The most active retail trading group (mid-frequency), although having the highest win rate across the entire network, is limited by capital efficiency and a lack of systemic advantages, resulting in a median actual return approaching zero.

  • Certainty trap: Betting on high-probability events (>0.8) faces an extremely asymmetrical risk-reward ratio (a small profit if you win, zero if you lose), with a negative long-term expected value.

  • Golden odds range: True alpha is highly concentrated in the price range of 0.2 - 0.4. This is the area with the greatest market divergence and the optimal profit-loss ratio (Odds).

  • Focus premium: Data proves that "all-round" traders struggle to survive.

  • Vertical experts who specialize in a few specific sectors typically earn four times the average return of diversified traders. Finding 1: Mid-frequency trading carries the highest risk. We categorize addresses into three tiers based on the number of trades: Low-frequency trading (Low): ~0.35 trades per day | Win rate ~40% Mid-frequency trading (Mid): ~3.67 trades per day | Win rate ~43% High-frequency/Ultra-frequency trading (High/Ultra): >14 trades per day | Win rate ~21-26% Superficially, mid-frequency traders seem to be the market leaders: highest win rate: reaching ~43%; lowest percentage of losing accounts: Loss Ratio only... 50.3%, far lower than the High group's 77.1%. This gives the illusion that as long as you maintain a moderate number of 3-4 trades per day, you can consistently profit. However, when we introduce PnL (Profit/Loss) data, the truth comes to light: Median PnL: The value for the median group is 0.001, almost zero. What does this mean? It means that for the vast majority of median traders, despite daily research, betting, and seemingly winning more than losing, your account equity remains stagnant. In contrast, while High Frequency (HF) and Ultra Frequency (UHF) have median losses (-0.30 and -1.76 respectively), their Mean Profit/Loss (MPL) is boosted to +922 or even +2717 by a very small number of top-performing addresses. This illustrates that the HF field is a "battleground for machines"—surviving on low win rates, high profit/loss ratios, and systematic strategies (such as market making and arbitrage), a model that ordinary people cannot replicate. Deep Attribution: Why Does Mid-Frequency Trading Fall into the "Mediocrity Trap"? Lacking Systematic Alpha, Becoming a "Coin Flipper": Mid-frequency traders are mostly active retail investors. A win rate of ~43% and a median return close to 0 indicate that the overall performance of this group is close to a random walk. They participate in the market based on intuition or fragmented information. While this avoids the significant drawdowns caused by strategy failures seen in high-frequency bots, it also fails to build a true competitive advantage. They are repeatedly participating in the market, rather than profiting from it. Survivorship bias masks tail risks: there is a huge gap between the average PnL (+915) and the median PnL (-0.001) of mid-frequency traders. This indicates extreme polarization within the mid-frequency group. A very small number of "experts" with core insider knowledge or exceptional judgment raise the average, while the remaining 50% or more are wasting their time. High-frequency trading is difficult to learn, and low-frequency trading is insufficient: ordinary users cannot imitate the systematic high-frequency strategies of the High/Ultra band (high technical threshold, low win rate, and high psychological pressure), yet they are unwilling to accept the extremely low trading frequency of the Low band. As a result, a large amount of capital and energy has been poured into the Mid-frequency band, making it the most crowded, fiercely competitive, and mediocre "red ocean." Practical Insight: Data tells us a harsh truth: if you simply become a "diligent mid-frequency trader," the result is likely to be a wasted effort. The real value lies not in imitating the average behavior of "mid-frequency" traders, but in identifying differences. Avoiding Pitfalls: The vast majority of mid-frequency addresses are simply performing Brownian motion, offering no copy trading value. Mining: The real Alpha is hidden at the right tail of the mid-frequency group—those extremely few who, at the same frequency, outperformed the "zero gravity." This is precisely the core value of our copy trading tool: helping you skip the trial-and-error phase of "long-term mid-frequency trading with no advantage," and using algorithms to directly identify the 1% of Alpha addresses that truly generate excess returns from a massive amount of mediocre mid-frequency denominators. Discovery Two: "Picking up pennies" and "buying lottery tickets" will eventually lead to nothing. We stratified traders' risk preferences according to their holding prices and discovered a harsh reality: whether you only buy "lottery tickets" (<0.2) or only buy "certainty" (>0.9), you will be a loser in the long run. We define three typical strategies: High-certainty strategy (Consensus Betting): Positions are concentrated at prices >0.9, focusing on events that are "almost certain." High-odds strategy (Long-shot Betting): Positions are concentrated at prices <0.2, betting on low-probability, low-risk events. Dynamic strategy: Positions are evenly distributed, not fixated on extreme odds. The data reveals a huge profit gap: Data Interpretation: The average return of hybrid strategies is 13 times that of high-certainty strategies. It's worth noting that the median return for all groups is ≤0. This means that even in the best-performing hybrid group, profits are highly concentrated in the hands of top players, and the vast majority of people do not even beat transaction fees. 1. Why does betting on "certainty" fail? Intuitively, buying into a "sure-win" strategy with a 0.95 bet seems to have very low risk. From a financial mathematics perspective, this is an extremely poor trade: **Extremely Asymmetric Downside Risk:** Entering at 0.95 means risking 1.0 of your principal for a 0.05 return. If a black swan event occurs (e.g., Biden suddenly withdrawing from the race, or a game being reversed in the last minute), the loss from a single event would require 19 consecutive successful trades to break even. In the long run, the probability of a black swan event is often higher than 5%. **Alpha Exhaustion (Priced In):** When the price reaches >0.9, market consensus has already formed. Entering at this point is essentially taking over for those who are already aware of the market's moves; there is no informational advantage to be gained. 2. The "Lottery Trap" of High-Odds Strategies Betting on low-probability events with odds <0.2 also performs poorly because: Overestimation Bias: Retail investors often overestimate their ability to predict "unpopular" events. In efficient predictive markets, prices usually already contain most of the implicit information. Long-term purchases of "lottery tickets" that are correctly priced by the market will inevitably result in the continuous erosion of capital. Low Capital Efficiency: Although the profit multiple per trade is high, the extremely low win rate will cause capital to remain in a state of drawdown for a long time, making it difficult to form a compounding effect. Actionable Insight: Reject "one-track mind" traders. When selecting copy trading targets, avoid addresses with extreme price distributions (all red or all green). True Alpha players are characterized by strategic flexibility—they bet on divergence at 0.3 and take profits at 0.8, rather than mechanically sticking to a particular odds range. Finding 3: The optimal risk range is 0.2-0.4. We stratified addresses by average purchase cost (Implied Probability) to find the "sweet spot" with the highest risk-adjusted returns. The data reveals a clear non-linear return distribution: true Alpha doesn't exist at the extremes, but is concentrated in the 0.2-0.4 price range. Comparison of performance across different price ranges: [link to image] In-depth insight: Why is 0.2-0.4 the most profitable range? 1. Capturing "Pricing Divergence" A buy price between 0.2 and 0.4 indicates that the market consensus believes the probability of the event occurring is only 20%-40%. Traders who consistently profit within this range are essentially engaging in "cognitive arbitrage." They can identify events underestimated by public sentiment (e.g., the market is overly pessimistic and misjudging a candidate's chances of a comeback). Compared to simply following the consensus (buying > 0.8), betting in the divergence zone, once validated, can yield explosive returns of 2.5 to 5 times. 2. The Perfect "Asymmetric Risk/Reward Structure" In the > 0.8 range (certainty trap): Investors face poor odds of "a small profit if they win, zero if they lose." As the data shows, the average return in this range is negative, and the win rate is only 19.5% (meaning that most people who buy > 0.8 ultimately perish due to black swan events).

  • In the 0.2-0.4 range (Alpha comfort zone): This is a range with "convexity." Downside risk is locked (principal), while upside potential is flexible. Excellent traders maximize profits in this range through the dual advantages of a high win rate (49.7%) and high odds.

  • Avoid the "lottery trap" (<0.2): Although the theoretical odds are highest in the extremely low price range, data shows that its performance is far inferior to the 0.2-0.4 range. This indicates that events below 0.2 are often true "garbage time" or pure noise, and excessively betting on extremely low probability events lacks statistically positive expected value.

Practical Insight: Pay attention to "divergence hunters".

Actionable Insight: Focus on "divergence hunters".

When selecting traders to copy, priority should be given to those whose average purchase price has consistently remained between 0.2 and 0.4. This data characteristic indicates that the account neither blindly chases high-risk lotteries nor tries to "pick up pennies" in low-odds consensus zones, but rather focuses on finding undervalued opportunities where market pricing has become ineffective. This is the core competency most worth replicating. Finding 4: Concentrated strategies outperform diversified strategies. We calculated the Focus Ratio (total number of transactions / number of markets participated in) for each address and categorized them into two types: Diversified strategy: Participates in a large number of markets, with fewer transactions per market. Concentrated strategy: Focuses on a few markets, with more transactions per market. Results show: The concentrated strategy yielded four times the return of the diversified strategy ($1,225 vs $306). It's worth noting that the concentrated strategy actually had a lower win rate ($33.8% vs 41.3%). The concentrated strategy achieved significant returns on a small number of high-odds opportunities. Explanation: In-depth research creates an advantage. Focusing on researching a few markets makes it easier to identify market pricing discrepancies, thus generating excess returns on a few trades. Win rate is not the key metric. What matters is not the number of wins, but the ratio of winning profits to losing losses. Concentrated strategies accept a lower win rate in exchange for higher returns per trade. Limitations of Diversification: Participating in too many markets leads to insufficient research depth in each market, making one more susceptible to market consensus and hindering the discovery of true alpha. Analogy: As Warren Buffett said, "Diversification is the self-protection of the ignorant." If one has an informational or judgmental advantage, one should concentrate on the few opportunities with the highest certainty. Lesson from Copy Trading: Prioritize traders who focus on specific types of markets (such as specific sports leagues, political events in specific countries, etc.). Their level of specialization often implies a deeper understanding and stronger predictive ability. II. Lessons for Copy Traders: How to Identify Real "Smart Money"? To quantify the level of specialization among traders, we constructed the Focus Ratio (Focus Ratio = Total Number of Trades / Number of Markets Participated) and divided traders into two distinct groups: **Generalists:** Participating broadly in a large number of markets, with low frequency of trades in a single market, attempting to reduce risk through diversification. **Specialists:** Focusing deeply on a few markets, repeatedly trading and adding to positions in a single market, exhibiting a strong "sniping" characteristic. The data reveals a striking "focus premium": Strategy Type Average Return (Avg PnL) Win Rate Number of Addresses Generalists $306 41.3% 68,016 Specialists $1,225 33.8% 22,458 Data Interpretation: The average return of concentrated strategies is 4 times that of diversified strategies. However, a highly misleading phenomenon emerges: the win rate of concentrated strategies (33.8%) is significantly lower than that of diversified strategies (41.3%). This reveals the true profit logic of advanced players in the prediction market. In-depth Attribution: Why "Less is More"? 1. Building a Moat Through Information Asymmetry (Information Edge) Prediction markets are essentially information games. Decentralized traders attempt to span multiple fields such as politics, sports, and crypto, resulting in a superficial understanding of any single market, making them vulnerable to exploitation. Centralized traders, on the other hand, cultivate a vertical information advantage by focusing on a single sector (e.g., studying only NBA player data or tracking polls in swing states). This depth allows them to detect subtle market pricing discrepancies. 2. Debunking the Win-Rate Fallacy Data shows that high returns are often accompanied by relatively low win rates. This is because concentrated experts tend to act when there are high odds/high divergence (e.g., buying at odds of 0.3), rather than picking up "certainty coins" > 0.9. Diversified: Wins many small bets (high win rate), loses one big bet (black swan event), ultimately resulting in mediocre returns. Concentrated: Can tolerate many small trial-and-error attempts (low win rate) in exchange for a few precise, high-risk, high-reward trades. This is typical venture capital (VC) logic, not the logic of working for someone else. 3. Validating Buffett's Logic in the Prediction Market As Buffett said, "Diversification is the self-protection of the ignorant." In the stock market, diversification is to avoid unsystematic risks; however, in the zero-sum game of the prediction market, diversification often means the dilution of attention. If you are confident that you have some edge, the best strategy is not to cast a wide net, but to concentrate your firepower on the few opportunities you are most confident in. Practical Insight: Find "vertical track experts." In copy trading, a high Focus Ratio is a more important indicator than a high win rate. Bad Signal: Avoid those "jacks-of-all-trades" who buy everything. Good Signal: Prioritize accounts active only under specific tags. For example, an address that only trades "US Election" and has a stable profit curve is far more valuable than an address that trades both "NBA" and "Bitcoin." The level of specialization directly determines the purity of the Alpha. III. From Data Insights to Practical Tools This report is not only a data review but also the underlying logic for building our Smart Copy-Trading system. To achieve long-term profitability on Polymarket, manually screening 90,000 addresses is unrealistic. We are encapsulating these exclusive data insights into an automated screening and risk control tool to solve the three most challenging problems in copy trading: 1. Intelligent Removal of Market Maker Noise: Current public rankings are riddled with market makers (MMs) and arbitrage bots that inflate trading volumes. Copying them not only fails to generate profits but can also lead to losses due to slippage. Solution: Utilizing proprietary order book analysis and trading characteristic recognition algorithms, we automatically filter out systematic market makers, focusing only on active traders who genuinely profit based on their insights. 2. Vertical Matching Based on "Focus": General "profit rankings" have limited value; you need experts in specific fields. Solution: Based on Focus Ratio and historical behavior, we assign high-precision "capability tags" (such as US elections, NBA sports events, and Crypto whales) to addresses. The system will accurately match you with vertical experts with informational advantages in the fields you follow. 3. Dynamic Style Drift Monitoring (Style Drift Detection) The most insidious risk of copy trading lies in the sudden failure of a trader's strategy or a sudden change in behavior. Solution: We have established a real-time risk control model. When a long-term stable address suddenly deviates from its historical behavioral characteristics (e.g., from low-frequency focus to high-frequency broad-based trading, or an abnormally large single risk exposure), the system will identify it as an abnormal signal and issue a timely warning to help users avoid drawdown risks. Conclusion & Product Beta Testing Prediction markets are a brutal zero-sum game. Data from 90,000 addresses proves that long-term winners succeed because they are extremely disciplined: focusing on specific areas and identifying pricing discrepancies. All the core metrics mentioned in this report (Focus Ratio, pricing range analysis, market maker elimination) are integrated into Hubble's data backend. Our initial motivation for building this tool was simple: to replace blind retail investor intuition with an institutional-grade data perspective. Beta Testing Application: Hubble's Polymarket intelligent copy trading tool is currently undergoing small-scale gray-scale testing. We will send you a beta testing invitation via private message. We hope this data-driven selection system can help you truly outperform the market.

data.title: PayPal Joins Google’s AI-Powered Checkout as Key Payment Option Through Universal Commerce Protocol
data.description:
PayPal To Become Key Payment Option In Google’s New AI-Driven Checkout

PayPal has confirmed it will integrate with Google’s Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP), an AI-powered framework designed to streamline interactions between consumers, retailers, and payment providers.

The company will soon appear as a payment choice within Google’s new checkout system, according to a press statement released over the weekend.

Excited to share @PayPal's support of @Google's Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP) as the next phase of our expanded partnership announced last fall.

This is a milestone moment making agentic commerce a reality for consumers.

Let's go @sundarpichai! 🚀 pic.twitter.com/9QCZo04n67

— Alex Chriss (@acce) January 12, 2026

How Will UCP Change Online Shopping

Google unveiled UCP during the National Retail Federation’s annual convention in New York, positioning it as an open, agentic commerce standard.

The protocol is designed to allow AI systems to assist with product discovery, checkout, and post-purchase support while enabling merchants and payment providers to interact seamlessly.

Vidhya Srinivasan, vice president and general manager of Google Ads & Commerce, explained:

“Instead of requiring unique connections for every individual agent, UCP enables all agents to interact easily.”

The protocol is platform-agnostic, aiming to reduce one-off integrations and support multiple credential providers.

UCP builds on Google’s previous agentic commerce protocols, including Agent Payments Protocol (AP2), Agent2Agent, and Model Context Protocol (MCP), and the company plans to expand its capabilities with loyalty programs, personalised recommendations, and tailored shopping experiences.

We’ve launched the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP), a new open standard for agentic commerce that works across the shopping journey!

UCP is compatible with A2A, AP2, and MCP, and was co-developed with partners like Etsy, Shopify, Wayfair, and Target → https://t.co/0kpbdUyKj0pic.twitter.com/shuBlZho83

— Google Cloud Tech (@GoogleCloudTech) January 12, 2026

Why PayPal Is Joining UCP

PayPal executives described the protocol as a way to remove barriers that have limited agentic commerce adoption.

Prakhar Mehrotra, SVP and Head of AI at PayPal, said:

“Protocols like UCP turn agentic commerce into something merchants can actually adopt at scale. Interoperability is what allows retailers to connect at once and reach many environments, while maintaining trust, transparency, and control.”

Michelle Gill, general manager of small business and financial services at PayPal, added:

“The next generation of commerce will be defined by how well we build open, trusted infrastructure that serves everyone. Supporting and collaborating with Google on UCP shows how a trusted payments experience layer makes agentic commerce a reality for consumers.”

PayPal has long acted as a reliable infrastructure layer for online payments, connecting hundreds of millions of consumers and tens of millions of merchants globally, while offering fraud protection and buyer-seller safeguards.

The company believes these foundations will be increasingly important as AI agents take on larger roles in shopping.

Who Helped Build UCP

UCP was developed with input from major retailers and e-commerce platforms, including Shopify, Etsy, Wayfair, Target, and Walmart.

AI agents will be a big part of how we shop in the not-so-distant future.

To help lay the groundwork, we partnered with Shopify, Etsy, Wayfair, Target and Walmart to create the Universal Commerce Protocol, a new open standard for agents and systems to talk to each other across… pic.twitter.com/8gDxDWjF9q

— Sundar Pichai (@sundarpichai) January 11, 2026

Vanessa Lee, vice president at Shopify, highlighted the company’s experience in building large-scale checkout systems:

“Shopify has a history of building checkouts for millions of unique retail businesses. We have taken everything we’ve seen over the decades to make UCP a robust commerce standard that can scale.”

Some analysts caution that agentic commerce may not succeed without active coordination between consumers, merchants, and payment providers.

Richard Crone, CEO of Crone Consulting, warned that if checkouts move off merchant sites, “the merchant loses the last touch point…The product detail pages are the fuel that they need to feed their agentic commerce engine.”

PayPal Expands Its European Presence

Alongside its UCP integration, PayPal has participated in a €12 million Series A funding round for Amsterdam-based payments firm Klearly, bringing the company’s total funding to €20 million.

Klearly serves over 4,000 merchants and plans to expand deeper into Italy and Belgium.

A year after €6m seed led via former @Mastercard CEO, Dutch hospitality #payments#startup#Klearly raises €12m Series A led by @PayPal's VC arm. Just 3 years old, offers agile, bar/restaurant focussed #payment layer that is hardware/ePOS agnostic. https://t.co/UPhZ9XeZ1mpic.twitter.com/7Gepx9FxIa

— Rob Fernandes (@rob2775) January 13, 2026

In the United States, PayPal has strengthened its physical presence in New York City with a 10-year lease covering 261,000 square feet at 345 Hudson Street and 555 Greenwich Street in Hudson Square, near Google and Disney offices.

345 Hudson Street (left) and 555 Greenwich Street (right) in Hudson Square

Will UCP Redefine Commerce Infrastructure

PayPal sees UCP as a chance to enhance trust and efficiency in the emerging agentic commerce landscape.

Ashish Gupta, VP/GM of Merchant Shopping at Google, stated:

“For agentic commerce to scale, it’s critical for the industry to align on a common set of standards. We are proud to have PayPal endorse the Universal Commerce Protocol as the foundation for that future.”

With the new integration, AI-driven shopping experiences could become more seamless, giving consumers smarter product suggestions while allowing merchants and payment providers to maintain oversight and reliability.
